draft1:
Self-driving cars have beneficial and disadvantageous effects on society. Although the benefits of self-driving cars may seem promising, the adverse effects might deter one from investing in them as the future of automobile technology. First, let’s review the positive side of self-driving cars. 

Self-driving cars would allow parents to feel safer about their high-school children “driving” to school with a reduced risk of speeding or an accident. If their child is distracted by a text, it wouldn’t matter because he/she wouldn’t need to pay attention to the road. If everyone had self-driving cars, there would be much fewer accidents, and speeding would be a thing of the past, allowing police officers more time and manpower to devote to other crimes. These self-driving cars would also benefit the sick and the elderly who would otherwise need to depend on others to get around. 

From an industrial growth prospective, the move toward self-driving cars would promote job growth for minds with a knack for technological advancement and machine learning. A new industry would be created. This can translate to the public transportation sector; self-driving buses and trains may soon follow. 

However, although there are many positives of self-driving cars, as with any technological advancements, there are also risks. Those with self-driving cars may need to worry about keeping their data secure from hackers who may want to know a person’s most frequently visited locations. This data could be used in targeted advertisements, or for more malicious purposes such as ascertaining the best place for a potential robbery or when the home would be empty. In adverse weather or road conditions that would provide the self-driving car with less-clear visual data, there could be a greater risk for an accident. If the occupants of the car do not pay careful attention, they may end up in a car accident. Even more alarming, if a self-driving car is on a slippery road and spins out of control, does it jerk out of the way to save a child pedestrian or the fit male pedestrian? The car wouldn’t know how to make these “gut-feeling” decisions. Additionally, if an accident does happen, where does the blame lie? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter. If the driver was not technically in control of the vehicle, they may not be responsible either. All that is left is the manufacturing company, however that doesn’t seem appropriate either. New legislation would need to be implemented to account for these possible scenarios.

Whereas some industries may see job growth, public transportation personnel would have a job reduction, as would taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially harming export sales from countries that would need it. If these self-driving cars are mostly electric, many gas stations may go out of business. 

Thus, although self-driving cars may provide some benefits to society, the overall risks and potential harm is far greater. People would lose the ability to do things for themselves, others may exploit holes in the technology’s security to harm others, and industries may collapse due to the sudden shift in technology that would ultimately change how the world functions.

These cars would have sensors on the outside of the vehicles that would allow them to coordinate movement in tight places. The cars could also perform parallel parking in close proximity to other cars, potentially providing more available parking spaces. Needing to find a parking space near an event would also be less necessary with self-driving cars. The car could drop a person off at his/her designated location, and then drive off to the nearest parking lot. From this perspective, this would eliminate the need for valet parking and save individuals money.
	From an industrial growth prospective, the move toward self-driving cars would promote job growth for minds with a knack for technological advancement and machine learning. A new industry would be created. This can translate to the public transportation sector; self-driving buses and trains may soon follow. There could be work on how to make these cars more efficient and economically friendly, moving away from gas-powered cars which have harmful effects on the environment.  
	However, although there are many positives of self-driving cars, as with any technological advancement, there are also risks. Those with self-driving cars may need to worry about keeping their data secure from hackers who may want to know a person’s most frequently visited locations. This data could be used in targeted advertisements, or for more malicious purposes such as ascertaining the best place for a potential robbery or when the home would be empty. Furthermore, what happens if someone hacks a car, takes control, and causes numerous accidents? Self-driving cars would provide a unique opportunity for those who have ill intent toward others to do so from behind a computer. Extra security precautions would need to be handled, offering yet another layer of obsequiousness to the mix. In cases in which a system is hacked and the vehicle is used to harm others, are the inventors behind the security system to some degree at fault?
Environmental conditions could also lead to error on the part of the self-driving car. In adverse weather or road conditions that would provide the self-driving car with less-clear visual data, there could be a greater risk for an accident if the occupants of the car do not pay careful attention. The self-driving cars may be worse than humans at detecting and avoiding potholes or black ice on the road. When these adverse conditions do occur, the self-driving car may only make calculated decisions, unable to account for the many scenarios the human mind can create and make decisions on based on emotional judgments. For example, if a self-driving car is on a slippery road and spins out of control, does it jerk out of the way to save a child pedestrian or the fit male pedestrian? The car wouldn’t know how to make these “gut-feeling” decisions. Additionally, if an accident does happen, where does the blame lie? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter. If the driver was not technically in control of the vehicle, they may not be responsible either. All that is left is the manufacturing company, however that doesn’t seem appropriate either. New legislation would need to be implemented to account for these possible scenarios. 
One reason people may promote self-driving cars is that they may reduce the consequences of being distracted in the car or intoxicated while driving. Some of these distractions are things outside of the car such as signs, events, or a car accident. Other distractions may be passengers in the car, reading an email, talking on the phone, or texting on the phone. Although self-driving cars may severely reduce the possibility of accidents due to distractions an inebriation, such as those mentioned above, there are other ways to reduce these risks. Laws to limit talking or texting with a cell-phone, less distracting billboards, and mandatory safety classes or tests every several years would also improve or maintain people's attentiveness while driving. Providing more rest-stops along highways for drowsy drivers would help the issue of fatigue. A breathalyzer could be installed in the car to require a person's blood-alcohol level to be below the legal limit before the car can start. This may also reduce people's feelings of "getting comfortable" with driving. Given this, although self-driving cars offer one, expensive, solution to the problem of being distracted while driving, they are not the only potential way to solve such a problem. 
	Finally, whereas some industries may see job growth, public transportation personnel would have a job reduction, as would taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially harming export sales from countries that would need it. If these self-driving cars are mostly electric, many gas stations may go out of business. 
	Thus, although self-driving cars may provide some benefits to society, they also come with just as many, if not more, risks to society. In some instances, they are not the only option for combating traffic accidents. For one, although this would be cutting edge technology, the risk for data security breaches also increases. Whereas people would no longer need to drive a car themselves, during an emergency they would be unable to due to a lack of knowledge. Additionally, for the self-driving car system to have the highest chance of working, there needs to be complete assimilation into a self-driving car world. Everyone would need a self-driving car to significantly reduce accidents due to human error. This spins into an ethical dilemma within itself, what happens to the people who refuse to own a self-driving car? Finally, although some job growth would occur to help implement the new technology, the eradication of gasoline-run and human operated cars would severely harm gas, oil, public transportation, and taxi industries. The sudden shift in technology would ultimately negatively change how the world functions.

Self-driving cars present both advantageous and disadvantageous effects on society. While the benefits of self-driving cars may seem promising, the potential adverse effects could deter one from investing in them as the future of automobile technology. This essay examines both the positive and negative aspects of self-driving cars to clarify my stance against their widespread adoption.

First, consider the positive side of self-driving cars. They would allow parents to feel safer about their high-school children “driving” to school with a reduced risk of speeding or accidents. If their child is distracted by a text, it wouldn’t matter because they wouldn’t need to pay attention to the road. With universal adoption of self-driving cars, accidents would significantly decrease, and speeding could become obsolete, freeing police officers to focus on other crimes. Additionally, these cars would greatly benefit the sick and elderly, who otherwise rely on others for transportation.

From an industrial growth perspective, self-driving cars would stimulate job growth for individuals skilled in technological advancement and machine learning, creating a new industry. This innovation could extend to public transportation, leading to self-driving buses and trains.

However, despite these positives, self-driving cars come with inherent risks. Owners may face threats to their data security from hackers interested in personal travel patterns, which could be exploited for targeted advertisements or more malicious purposes, such as planning robberies. In adverse weather or road conditions, self-driving cars may struggle with unclear visual data, increasing the risk of accidents. If passengers are inattentive, they may find themselves involved in a collision. More troubling is the ethical dilemma in emergency situations: if a self-driving car must choose between hitting a child or an adult pedestrian, it lacks the ability to make such instinctive decisions. Furthermore, if an accident occurs, determining liability becomes complex. The car itself cannot be held accountable for vehicular manslaughter, and if the driver is not in control, they may not be responsible, leaving the manufacturing company as a potential scapegoat. New legislation would be necessary to address these scenarios.

While some industries might benefit from job growth, others could experience job loss, such as public transportation workers and taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially affecting export sales from oil-dependent countries. If self-driving cars are primarily electric, many gas stations may go out of business.

In conclusion, although self-driving cars offer several societal benefits, the overall risks and potential harms are significant. People risk losing their ability to perform tasks independently, security vulnerabilities could be exploited for harm, and industries might collapse due to the technological shift that would fundamentally alter global operations. Therefore, despite the allure of innovation, the cautious approach is to reconsider the widespread adoption of self-driving cars.

As technology continues to progress at a rapid pace, innovators have begun to explore the possibility of self-driving cars. What once seemed like a child’s fantasy of a futuristic dystopian society is now becoming a reality. The impact that these vehicles may have on society is immense. Self-driving cars promise a leisurely driving experience, eliminating the stress associated with manual driving by taking over all driving responsibilities. They could potentially limit accidents by removing human error from the equation, as they are programmed to avoid accidents and obey traffic laws. However, this is an idealistic perspective. Self-driving cars also come with significant negative impacts. While they may remove the human error from driving, they introduce the possibility of errors in programming. Algorithms could be miscalculated, or the vehicle's technology could be faulty, posing dangers similar to those of an average human driver. Therefore, it would not be beneficial to society to push for their implementation.

The risks associated with self-driving cars are too significant to ignore. Proponents argue that these vehicles will not be distracted drivers, as they follow set algorithms on the road. While it is true that human drivers face numerous distractions, they possess instincts and reactions that machines cannot replicate. The decision-making capabilities of self-driving cars may theoretically surpass those of humans, but they cannot replace human judgment. Consider a scenario where a self-driving car encounters a shopping cart rolling into its path. The algorithms might direct the car to swerve, but what if there is a person crossing the street next to the cart? The car might not discern that it would be better to brace for the cart's impact than to endanger a human life. In contrast, a human driver could consciously decide to avoid the pedestrian, demonstrating an instinct that self-driving cars lack.

This scenario is just one of many that highlight the limitations of self-driving cars. They lack the human instinct and decision-making ability that no algorithm can replicate. Given the unknowns and high risks associated with self-driving cars, it is prudent and safest to avoid their implementation, as their potential negative outcomes outweigh the benefits they might offer to society.

As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with the implementation of that technology. This is also true with autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With the testing of self-driving cars currently happening in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether or not autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of the public health and wellness debate in the media. Many cars already come equipped with some of the basic technology - autonomous breaking, self-parking, or sensors that detect close objects, so we may be closer to the future than we think.

There are several pros for the use of self-driving cars, and one of the biggest is safety. While a small percentage of car accidents could be caused by mechanical failures, the majority are caused by a distracted driver. A computer driver would take the human element factor out of the equation and remove most of the danger just by being a computer. Computers make for the ideal driver as they will not get tired, lose their eyesight, get distracted, imbibe in drugs or alcohol, etc. Computers also use specific algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other collected information that help to dramatically decrease the chance of an accident. 

In addition to safety, there are many other positive side-effects to autonomous driving. Self-driving cars could help with traffic optimization, as the cars could communicate with each other to help identify traffic problems and their specific algorithms and sensors could help to reduce commute times while also allowing more cars on the road with less traffic. People with disabilities would have more options at their disposal. Police could focus less time on traffic stops and more time on other crimes. People would have more time to do other thigns with less parking and DMV headaches for drivers. The benefits are endless. 

With all the good things that come with technology, there are bound to be some bad things, as well. One of the biggest downfalls anticipated with self-driving cars would be their affect on the economy. Not only is the cost of implementing the new technology more expensive than most Americans can afford, it could also eliminate a lot of jobs in transportation. It is also expected that since most self-driving cars are powered by computers, that they would be electric cars, further putting a strain on the gas industry. Reliance on technology could make a driver's license, insurance, the DMV, and other driving related vendors obsolete. The benefits of self-driving cars would also depend upon more people opting into the option. 

Other downfalls to autonomous cars would be the flaws with technology itself. A computer cannot detect a road block or read human hand signals, so road construction would be an obstacle. The sensors are located on the outside of the vehicle, so severe weather can damage them. Computers attract hackers and software requires user information, so there is always a chance for breach of security. Another risk would be the technology failure where a person must take over the controls - what if it is someone that has never driven their own car before? What if the GPS goes down? How will a person be able to get to where they need to go?

While a completely autonomous driving society is probably a ways off, society certainly seems to be moving towards it. I do believe that self-driving cars would be a benefit in many ways - safety, inclusion, environment, and time/traffic - but I think we have some questions that need to be answered before we get there. While there may be jobs that are lost in transportation, there will be jobs that are created with the technology and manufacturing of these vehicles. The inclusion of people with disabilities, the elderly, and those that do not have a license is a powerful tool for those that were previously reliant on others. In my opinion, self-driving cars will get over the hump and be a part of the near future, at least in a small realm.

As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with its implementation. This is particularly true for autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With the testing of self-driving cars currently underway in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of public health and wellness discussions. Many cars already come equipped with basic technology—such as autonomous braking, self-parking, or sensors that detect nearby objects—so the future may be closer than we think.

I am in favor of self-driving cars, as they offer several advantages, with safety being the most significant. While a small percentage of car accidents result from mechanical failures, the majority are caused by distracted drivers. A computer driver would eliminate the human factor and most of the danger, simply by being a computer. Computers make ideal drivers because they do not get tired, lose their eyesight, become distracted, or consume drugs or alcohol. They also use specific algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other factors, dramatically decreasing the likelihood of an accident.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars offer other positive side effects. They could optimize traffic flow, as the cars could communicate with each other to identify and resolve traffic issues. Their algorithms and sensors could reduce commute times while allowing more cars on the road with less congestion. People with disabilities would have more transportation options. Police could spend less time on traffic stops and more on other crimes. People could use their time more productively, with fewer parking and DMV hassles. The benefits are extensive.

However, with the advantages of technology come potential drawbacks. One of the biggest anticipated issues with self-driving cars is their economic impact. The cost of implementing new technology is currently beyond what most Americans can afford, and it could eliminate many jobs in transportation. As most self-driving cars are expected to be electric, this could strain the gas industry. Reliance on technology might render driver's licenses, insurance, the DMV, and other driving-related industries obsolete. The benefits of self-driving cars would also depend on widespread adoption.

Other potential issues include the limitations of the technology itself. Computers cannot detect roadblocks or interpret human hand signals, making road construction problematic. The sensors, located outside the vehicle, could be damaged by severe weather. Computers are vulnerable to hacking, and software requires user information, posing security risks. There is also the risk of technological failure, requiring a person to take control—what if that person has never driven a car before? What if the GPS fails? How would someone reach their destination?

While a completely autonomous driving society is likely some distance away, society is certainly moving toward it. I believe self-driving cars would be beneficial in many ways—enhancing safety, inclusivity, environmental sustainability, and traffic management—but there are questions that need answers before we fully embrace them. While jobs may be lost in transportation, new opportunities will arise in the technology and manufacturing sectors. The inclusion of people with disabilities, the elderly, and those without licenses is empowering for those previously dependent on others. In my opinion, self-driving cars will overcome these challenges and become a part of our near future, at least in a limited capacity.

The article on self-driving cars presents a thought-provoking discussion on the potential benefits and challenges of this emerging technology. One of the primary advantages of self-driving cars is their potential to significantly reduce the number of accidents caused by human error. Countless tragic incidents occur because individuals under the influence choose to drive. While services like Uber and Lyft offer alternatives, many individuals still risk driving themselves. Self-driving cars could mitigate this risk by removing the need for intoxicated individuals to make responsible decisions about transportation.

However, the introduction of self-driving cars raises complex logistical and ethical questions. A significant concern is determining responsibility in the event of an accident. For instance, if a child unexpectedly darts in front of a self-driving car and the car fails to stop in time, who is at fault? Furthermore, legal disputes could arise regarding whether the manufacturer is to blame for potential detection failures or if the human occupant should have been paying attention. If drivers are expected to remain fully attentive, this could contradict the benefit of allowing individuals under the influence to use their vehicles safely.

Upon considering these arguments, I assert that while self-driving cars offer certain advantages, they should not entirely replace human drivers. The goal should be to enhance safety and reduce accidents, but human drivers should remain fully engaged and attentive. Driving conditions often require rapid decision-making, which can be crucial in preventing accidents. A self-driving car may provide a false sense of security, potentially leading to complacency and less caution. Therefore, I believe that even with the advent of self-driving technology, drivers must be held accountable for accidents unless a genuine manufacturing error is involved. The ultimate responsibility for safe vehicle operation should rest with humans.

output_text:
Advocating for Self-Driving Cars: A Step Towards Progress

The advent of self-driving cars represents a significant leap forward in technology and a promising step towards a safer, cleaner, and more sustainable future. By embracing this innovation, we can dramatically reduce traffic accidents caused by human error and move away from the environmentally damaging gasoline industry. While the transition may pose challenges, such as job displacement, the benefits far outweigh the drawbacks, marking a necessary evolution in transportation.

Self-driving cars hold the potential to significantly decrease the number of traffic accidents, which are predominantly caused by human error. Studies indicate that human mistakes account for 94% of traffic accidents, with distractions such as texting, eating, or dealing with children and pets often diverting a driver's attention from the road. Unlike humans, the computer systems in autonomous vehicles are not susceptible to distractions, fatigue, or impairment from substances like alcohol. Consequently, self-driving cars can effectively address issues like distracted driving, reckless driving, and other dangerous behaviors, making our roads safer for everyone.

In addition to enhancing safety, self-driving cars are likely to be electric, presenting a substantial advantage for the environment. Electric vehicles (EVs) are known for their efficiency and minimal pollution compared to traditional gasoline-powered vehicles. With their quiet and smooth operation, EVs offer a modern alternative to the clunky and outdated gas-operated cars. Moreover, they are cheaper to operate due to lower fuel and maintenance costs. By eliminating tailpipe emissions, electric cars contribute to improved air quality, tackling the pressing issue of air pollution and reducing health risks associated with gasoline exposure, such as cancer and respiratory problems.

The shift towards electric and autonomous vehicles necessitates a transformation in the gasoline industry, which has long been a significant source of pollution. Transitioning away from fossil fuels is crucial, given their finite nature and the environmental harm they cause. While concerns about job losses in the gasoline industry are valid, history shows us that adaptation is possible. Just as society transitioned from horse-drawn carriages to automobiles, so too can we adapt to new technologies. Mechanics can be retrained to work on electric vehicles, and new opportunities will arise in the growing fields of renewable energy and technology.

Addressing potential job displacement is essential, but it should not hinder progress. The transition to self-driving cars will create new industries and job opportunities, ensuring that those affected by the decline of the gasoline industry can find alternative employment. Public transportation systems will still require drivers, and the demand for ride-sharing services is likely to increase, providing further job opportunities.

In conclusion, the integration of self-driving and electric vehicles promises a future with fewer accidents, less pollution, and more sustainable energy consumption. While the transition may pose challenges, the societal and environmental benefits make this a necessary and worthwhile pursuit. Embracing self-driving cars is not just a technological advancement; it is a step towards a safer and cleaner world.

Self driving cars are a highly debated topic simply because they pose virtually equal amounts of advantages and disadvantages. They offer more efficient transportation, mobility for all people, theoretically safer roads, and overall convenience by improving quality of life. In contrast, they also threaten jobs for those who work in transportation, pose danger in the event of a malfunction, are unable to understand human intention, and rely on somewhat faulty GPS systems. With such important advantages and disadvantages one has to ask if the risk is worth it. Society does not really need self driving cars, but given the direction of technological advances of late, self driving cars seem like a logical next step. The whole point of the digital age essentially is to improve life for everyone and self driving cars have enough potential to do that. Self driving cars are beneficial to society because their potential benefits outweigh the solvable costs they pose. 

The benefits of self driving cars are so wonderful that they diminish the argument to be made against self driving cars. It's no secret that humans are consumed by technology and appear to not get enough of it. The evidence lies in the fact that we can not seem to stay focused on the road for the duration of a car ride without checking our phones for insignificant messages and calls. These seemingly innocent phone checks while driving end up claiming the lives of thousands per year despite laws against texting or calling while on the road. Take Maryland for example, a state with some of the strictest laws against using a cell phone while driving yet it still ranks as one of the highest states for the worst drivers and most fatal accidents. People will continue to break the law as they please, but a computer will not. The computer in a self driving car can not be distracted as shown in pro number 3. I've read and heard numerous news stories about people driving and all of a sudden their breaks do not work causing them to crash into other drivers. In addition, malfunctions can be easily prevented if not at least securely managed if car companies are forced to offer routine check ups and software updates for the self driving cars. Essentially all of the cons about self driving cars are solvable and preventable problems that fail to overcast a permanent reluctance for their existence. Take for example the argument that self driving cars can not interpret human traffic signals. Embed a software that allows the cars to recognize when a light is out and a human is directing traffic. The car can analyze the human or ask the human in the car what it should do. Of course this is no easy feat but there are enough intelligent people in the world to figure this issue out and others just like it. From being better for the environment to reducing traffic and increasing mobility for the elderly and disabled, self driving cars have countless benefits that society seems to be passing up on.

Self driving cars present more benefits than costs, therefore, they should be implemented in the technology of tomorrow. If more information is shared about self driving cars and how their potential costs can be managed and prevented more people will be willing to make the switch and live a safer, easier life. The decision is simple, the technology exists, the only real debate here is whether society is ready to finally start living in the "future".

All these factors strongly support adopting this new way of driving. If all these people are safely driven to their destinations, we can imagine how grateful they would be for self-driving cars.

output_text:
Society is continuously evolving and adapting with regard to its technology. Products available to the public today would have been unimaginable just a few years ago. One futuristic concept currently being tested is the idea of self-driving cars. This technology appeals to society for numerous reasons: accessibility, perceived reduction in accidents, predicted traffic efficiency, and more. However, there are significant drawbacks to implementing self-driving cars within society, such as cost, program malfunctions, potential decline in driving skills, and the threat to jobs related to driving. Despite the potential benefits of self-driving cars, the disadvantages ultimately outweigh the advantages for society.

According to the article, a major motivation for developing self-driving cars is the belief that they will significantly reduce road accidents. However, many factors need to be perfectly aligned for this to be true. Passengers still need to know how to operate the car's mechanics to avoid accidents, and with human error being a possibility, the system is inherently flawed in achieving accident-free driving. Additionally, adverse weather conditions can interfere with the car’s laser sensors, requiring the passenger to take control, thus introducing the risk of human error once again. Cars cannot be fully programmed with every single local driving law, potential road hazard, or construction detour. Currently, GPS programs are not always accurate, but manual driving can compensate for this; if a self-driving car lacks this information, it could lead to accidents. There is no guarantee that self-driving cars can significantly reduce accidents. Moreover, these cars would complicate legal proceedings when accidents do occur, as they inevitably will. Who would be held responsible? The car? The manufacturer? The passenger?

Self-driving cars also pose significant societal challenges. For one, most people will not be able to afford them. Current estimates for the costs of engineering, power, and other expenses start at $100,000. To benefit even slightly from self-driving cars, they would need to be widely adopted, which seems impractical. Furthermore, their implementation could render many jobs obsolete. Auto manufacturers, driver’s education companies, personal injury lawyers, and the gasoline industry would face significant challenges. Many could lose their jobs, negatively impacting the unemployment rate and the economy.

Technology is a tremendous asset in modern society, but only when applied where it is truly needed. Would you want to live in a world where people have lost the ability to operate a car manually? A society so dependent on technology that a glitch or recall could leave them stranded, unable to travel due to their reliance on a self-driving car? An innovation that results in the loss of many jobs for working Americans? Why contend with these challenges when manually driving a car could eliminate so many economic, moral, and safety issues?

While the idea of self-driving cars is intriguing and offers some advantages, the potential risks and societal costs are too significant to ignore. The drawbacks—ranging from economic impacts to safety concerns—highlight the need for a cautious approach to this technological advancement.

Technological advances have significantly enhanced our society's convenience and efficiency in many areas, such as social networking, shopping, and personalized online streaming services. However, there is ongoing debate about whether these technologies improve or detract from society's well-being. Among these innovations, self-driving cars stand out as a pivotal development, promising safer transportation through sophisticated algorithms. While concerns about their potential risks, such as causing accidents, persist, I argue that self-driving technology offers substantial benefits, particularly for individuals facing various challenges, and can continuously improve through data-driven refinements.

First, self-driving cars can profoundly impact not only the general population but also individuals who face difficulties driving, such as those with disabilities, pregnant women, and others with similar challenges. These individuals often rely on public transportation or assistance for commuting, shopping, and traveling, which can be particularly burdensome in areas with underdeveloped public transit systems or in bustling urban centers. Self-driving cars present a superior, personalized transportation alternative for those unable to drive or who struggle with existing public transit options. Despite the risks associated with self-driving cars, such as difficulties operating safely in diverse weather and traffic conditions, these risks may not exceed those posed by human drivers, who can be distracted, intoxicated, or fatigued. Given that driving inherently involves risk, embracing self-driving technology could offer better transportation options for individuals who find current alternatives inadequate.

Moreover, the risks linked to self-driving cars can be mitigated through data collection and analysis. A key advantage of these vehicles is their ability to record data during accidents, enabling precise identification of causes. This data can then be used to enhance algorithms, reducing the likelihood of similar accidents in the future. In contrast, human error-related accidents often lack clear, actionable insights, and even when identified, corrective measures such as educational programs or policy changes take time to implement and yield results.

In conclusion, the adoption of self-driving cars holds the promise of new opportunities for those who face challenges with driving and alternative transportation methods. As data is collected and analyzed, the risks associated with self-driving technology can be minimized, potentially reducing the frequency of car accidents and offering an improved transportation experience for all.

In our rapidly developing society, new technology has an impact on so many new industries, especially in transportation. Currently, many cars have features such as motion sensors and autonomous braking. As these features become more prevalent, there is a question of whether self-driving cars will eventually take over the roads. Self-driving cars are determined to be safer than human driven cars due to the removal of human error. They are also proven to be more efficient because the computer has the driving responsibility, and more accessible to those who are not able to drive cars themselves. Unfortunately, there are some disadvantages to self-driving cars. Computers are unable to respond to human signals and unpredictable situations, which may be a serious safety concern. The use of self-driving cars may also have a negative effect on the economy because it eliminates many jobs such as taxi drivers, personal injury lawyers, and drivers ed instructors. However, the advantages in safety, efficiency, and accessibility of self-driving cars outweigh these possible risks and demonstrate that self-driving cars would be a very beneficial addition to our society.

Safety is the most notable advantage of self-driving cars. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors and instead use precise algorithms to drive, this greatly reduces the risk of accidents. Self-driving cars, however, have some disadvantages that human driven cars do not, such as risk of malfunction in certain weather conditions and inability to follow human traffic signals. There is also a question of what the driver may have to do if the technology malfunctions. However, these are all situations that occur infrequently. The situations in which self-driving cars would save lives occur much more frequently, showing that the safety advantages outweigh the possible risks.

Self-driving cars also have many advantages when it comes to efficiency in cost and time. With fewer accidents on the road, money will be saved for insurance and healthcare. Drivers are also able to use time on the road to complete other tasks since they don’t have to focus on the road. In large groups, self-driving cars participate in platooning. The cars are able to communicate with each other, which helps to reduce traffic and hazardous situations on the road. This saves time, gasoline, and also allows cars to travel at higher speeds, further saving driving time. Overall, the efficiency of self-driving cars reduces traffic and makes the road safer.
	
Finally, the accessibility of self-driving cars gives access to transportation to those who cannot drive cars themselves, such as disabled and elderly individuals. Disabled individuals often rely on others for transportation or have to use public transportation, greatly inhibiting their mobility. Access to self-driving cars would allow these individuals to travel in a more convenient way, granting them more freedom. This is true for elderly individuals as well. People often worry about allowing their elderly parents drive at a certain age, concerned about their safety but not wanting to take away their independence. With self-driving cars, this would not be an issue.
	
Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology increases safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

The cars are able to communicate with each other, which helps to reduce traffic and hazardous situations on the road. This saves time, gasoline, and also allows cars to travel at higher speeds. Overall, the efficiency of self-driving cars reduces traffic and makes the road a safer place. 
	Self-driving cars also give access to transportation to those who cannot drive cars themselves, such as disabled and elderly individuals. Disabled individuals often rely on others for transportation or have to use public transportation, greatly inhibiting their mobility. Access to self-driving cars would allow these individuals to travel in a more convenient way, granting them more freedom. This is true for elderly individuals as well. People often worry about allowing their elderly parents to drive at a certain age, concerned about their safety but not wanting to take away their independence. With self-driving cars, this would not be an issue since the cars drive themselves. 
	Despite these benefits, many are still skeptical of self-driving cars. Using new technology can be risky, since new challenges often arise. For example, self-driving cars have a greater chance of malfunction in certain weather conditions, and are also unable to follow human traffic signals such as a police officer directing traffic. As humans become more accustomed to self-driving cars, they may begin to lose the skills necessary to operate a vehicle. Becoming too reliant on technology may be an issue, as the car could malfunction, and the driver may be in a more dangerous situation than if they were operating a human driven car. While these concerns are certainly notable, the safety advantages of self-driving cars offset the possible risks. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors, the chance of an accident is greatly reduced. Computers are able to make calculations in order to stop a safe distance away from other objects, decreasing the probability of a crash. Self-driving cars will save many lives and should be considered in spite of the possible concerns. 
	As technology continues to advance, there are many new possibilities of improvement in transportation. Self-driving cars may sound like an invention from the future, but they will likely be on the roads sooner than expected. Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology improves safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

draft1:
I marvel at the ingenuity of our scientists and engineers in bringing us so close to the possibility of self-driven vehicles. 

Furthermore, I am a free spirit who cherishes her independence, including the freedom to go where she pleases when she pleases, without having to rely on other people, their schedules, their preferences and especially their notions about what I, as a woman of a certain age, should or should not be doing. All this makes me deeply empathetic toward seniors and the disabled who may not be able to drive, and for their sakes, I can deeply appreciate the value of a self-driven vehicle.

Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could mean the end of our reliance on fossil fuels,  something deeply to be desired by all of us who are worried about the future of our polluted planet.

And yet, I find that something deeper, perhaps even less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driven, at least for the present.

We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. In cases where computers are absolutely better than us at certain things, e.g. the analysis of large quantities of crucial health-related data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But 
I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them, in areas where they are not particularly better than we are. Driving is a case in point. You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 

And then, there is the questions of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation?  You may argue that we are everyday and everywhere rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, nor can the cars of anyone else, and you're all,  every single one of you, headed for disaster with no recourse, where at least if you were at the wheel you could have attempted some solution. It is this removal of human agency that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which 
can be the source of virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. I do not wish to be thus reduced.

In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 
          Some claim that self-driven cars would obviate the need for public transportation, seeing this as something to recommend them. I disagree. Apart from the jobs lost, which I think most would see as a negative, there is the potential loss of public space, which after all, is what a bus, a subway car, or a train is. Like cities, where they are most in demand, forms of public transportation make for chance encounters with others whom we may otherwise never encounter. Cities, especially the larger ones, are rich in human variety, and one way to ensure that we limit our contact with this enriching variety is to sequester us in our individual self-driven vehicles. Sequestration of a sort is already far advanced. Most of us walk around mesmerized by screens and tuned in to our own individual soundscapes delivered via headphone. At home, this pattern of behavior continues, sadly. We are losing the ability to be with each other, whether that means having rich and complex and spontaneous interactions or simply occupying the same space, something that is not as mindless and mundane and trivial as it might appear, as psychologists and neuroscientists are beginning to understand. Self-driven cars, by eliminating the need for public transportation will only deepen this social atomization.
          The advocates of autonomous vehicles claim that eliminating public transportation cuts infrastructure costs, and they might even say, it would make for greener environs. But it is not clear whether the rise in the number of autonomous vehicles will not require comparable infrastructure, as the majority must drive such vehicles if they are to be a feasible option in the first place.
         And speaking of majorities, we are brought face to face with another problem: cost. It will surely be a while before self-driven cars are within the means of the majority. And assuming that they could become a viable proposition before the majority of the population owns them, they would in the interim be status symbols, affordable only by the wealthy.  Our already deeply divided society can do without such obvious markers of status differentials.
	And then, there is the question of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation? 
	You may argue that we are daily rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. 
	I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, something as simple as a failure of traffic signals or a sudden thunderstorm, never mind a hacked program or a spectacular terrorist attack. Nor can the cars of anyone else. And you're all headed for disaster with no recourse. Rendered utterly passive where once you might have attempted some solution, no matter its efficacy. 
	It is this removal of human agency, individual and collective,  that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but rather thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which can breed virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. 
	I do not wish to be thus reduced.

As someone who cherishes independence and autonomy, I empathize deeply with seniors and individuals with disabilities who may not be able to drive. For their sake, I can appreciate the value of self-driving cars, which could offer newfound freedom without reliance on others’ schedules or preferences. Additionally, the prospect of self-driving cars being electric could signal a significant reduction in our reliance on fossil fuels, a necessary shift for those of us concerned about our planet's future.

While often perceived as routine or tedious, driving encompasses an array of competencies: maintaining attentiveness and alertness in dynamic situations, the ability to navigate traffic collaboratively, and ensuring the safety of all road users, including cyclists and pedestrians. In our increasingly fragmented world, these are crucial skills we cannot afford to lose. More is at stake than we might initially perceive.

Moreover, there is the critical issue of safety. Computer algorithms are not infallible; they can be compromised by viruses, hardware malfunctions, or hacking. What would happen if a rider, especially one not technologically savvy, found themselves in a complex and fluid scenario? Today's society is seeing the start of a new era in technology, where artificial intelligence is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. This includes the gradual integration of self-driving cars, or cars that rely on computer programming to give it directions.

This can be a great improvement to driving safety, as this is potentially a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible -- they can get distracted, feel sleepy, become under the influence -- and drastically increase their chance of accidents. Computers, however, do not succumb to such mistakes.

Additionally, they are able to communicate with other computers easily, so if there were multiple self-driving cars on the road, they can all sense each other within seconds and know how to navigate from there.

However, computers are not 100% fool-proof. They are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on more people to "opt-in" and drive their own self-driving car, otherwise, accidents will still happen.

But the thing is, dear reader, that all these cons can be remedied with time. We are currently seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation, but the idea of relying on new technology to propel us forward in society is a long-standing tradition. This is a natural process.

The more we work on this technology, the safer it will become. We will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. We will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily, or at all. And the more we improve upon it, the more widespread the technology will be, which will cause its pricepoint to decrease and in turn make self-driving cars much more accessible. 

There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology and then it decides to stop working, or it turns against us?" To that, dear reader, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we can use self-driving cars does not mean we will stop learning how to drive.

But, dear reader, I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever.

In today's society, we are seeing the start of a new era in technology where artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. Examples of this can be found in our homes, social media, everyday retail, and other facets of our lives. 
	Many dystopian books and films would like you to believe that the steady integration of AI is the start of our societal downfall, but this is simply not the case--take self-driving cars for example. For those of you who are unfamiliar, self-driving cars are vehicles that rely on AI to give itself directions and to literally drive itself without any human interaction. 
	In general, this is a great improvement to driving safety as it can provide a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible--they get distracted, they get sleepy, and they can choose to drive while under the influence--which can drastically increases the chance of accidents. Computers, however, are literally unable to succumb to such mistakes and are therefore safer. 
	Additionally, computers are able to communicate with other computers easily, unlike humans who have no form of communication with any of the other drivers in close proximity. This advantage allows computers in self-driving cars to sense each other within seconds and negotiate the road together. 
	However, many people think that if we were to rely on these AI-driven cars, it would become a gradual demise for us. Self-driving cars are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on people en masse to "opt-in" and buy their own self-driving cars, otherwise a single self-driving car cannot communicate with a vehicle which cannot "talk back."
	But what these naysayers are forgetting is that all of these downfalls can be remedied with time--we are only seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation. It is a natural process to introduce new technology into society and wait for newer models to come out until it is perfected. By dismissing the new technology in its early stages without giving it the chance to improve with time would be like telling a child to stop practicing at something because they were awful in the beginning. 
	Here is a simple example of how we can slowly rely on technology to assist us: traffic lights. Way before we relied on traffic lights to help guide us through intersections, we had traffic guards to help us. But over time, we allowed ourselves to accept traffic lights, instead of a human, to help us through. And of course, the traffic light wasn't perfect or even similar to the version we have today! It also had a simpler code and fewer systems to help us, but we let it stay up and now we have near-perfect traffic light controls and coordination systems. 
	The point is, the more we work on this technology, the safer it will become, but only if we give it a chance to grow and perfect itself. This is not to say that the negative points aren't valid or accurate observations on the current self-driving cars we have today. But we tend to forget that this is not an indication of what it could be. Of what it could provide for us. And how helpful and much safer it could actually be for us in the long-term. 
        Manufacturers, scientists, and computer scientists alike will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. Will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily. And with further improvement, the more common the technology will become and cause its pricepoint to decrease, which in turn will make self-driving cars much more accessible and allow a wider population to purchase their own.
	There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology, and then it decides to just stop working or turn against us?" To that, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot feature in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we give cars the knowledge of the road does not mean that all of a sudden it is going to drive off without us.
	But I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever, and if you are one of the few who miss such a time, then maybe self-driving cars were never meant for you to begin with.

In today's society, we are witnessing the dawn of a new technological era where artificial intelligence is becoming increasingly prevalent. This includes the gradual integration of self-driving cars, vehicles that use advanced computer programming to navigate.

Self-driving cars could significantly enhance driving safety, as they offer a potentially more secure mode of transport. Human drivers are prone to errors due to distractions, fatigue, or impairment, which can drastically increase the likelihood of accidents. In contrast, computers are not susceptible to these human weaknesses.

Additionally, autonomous vehicles can communicate seamlessly with one another. If multiple self-driving cars are on the road, they can detect each other within seconds and coordinate their movements, reducing the risk of collisions.

However, autonomous vehicles are not without flaws. They are vulnerable to hacking, can be deceived, and their efficacy relies on widespread adoption. Without a critical mass of self-driving cars, accidents may still occur.

Nonetheless, these challenges can be addressed over time. We are at the outset of this new phase in automation, but the concept of leveraging technology to advance society is a time-honored tradition. Progress in this area follows a natural trajectory.

As we continue to develop this technology, it will become safer. New software updates can enhance security against hacking. Improvements in spatial awareness and adaptability will make the systems less susceptible to being deceived. As technology advances, it will become more widespread, driving down costs and making self-driving cars more accessible to the general public.

There is also apprehension about the potential for a dystopian future. Some worry, "What if we become overly dependent on technology, and it fails us or turns against us?" To this, I assert that science fiction remains separate from reality. Just as the advent of calculators did not eliminate the teaching of mathematics, and autopilot systems in planes have not replaced pilot training, the adoption of self-driving cars will not eradicate the need for learning to drive.

One thing is for certain: the era of the stick shift may indeed be on its way to becoming a relic of the past.

draft1:
Society remains divided on the issue of self-driving cars. Some people think they’re great: they should reduce accidents, free up drivers’ time, and make obsolete the need for detailed knowledge of driving, thus letting students learn other subjects in school and allowing people who could never use cars alone in the past (like the disabled and elderly) more freedom. Meanwhile, others focus on the problems. Computers would not make decisions and adapt in the same manner as attentive human drivers, provide more security risks, and present a large potential for vehicle failure. I side with this latter group, and my main reasoning is that there are more weaknesses and things that can go wrong with a computer than with a human.
I’ve got a little story on computers who’ve been trained to act like they can drive better than humans can. In April my parents and I were in Florida visiting my grandparents. We had a rental car and my father was driving on the highway. A car in front of us was going far below the speed limit, so my father punched the accelerator in order to pass it. The rental car’s computer determined that he must have meant to hit the brakes and hit the accelerator by mistake. So, the car suddenly locked up on the busy highway and stopped, causing all passengers to be thrown forward and the car behind us to nearly rear-end us. There was no human error involved here. No distracted driving. Just a system that has been trusted and deployed into millions of cars on the road, activating without the driver’s knowledge or consent. This “feature” is just a taste of what interacting with full-blown self-driving cars would be like.
Autonomous vehicles pose even more risks when we move beyond considering bad software that only influences how the car interacts with the driver and begin to think about security and how the computer handles driving in varying environmental conditions and around other drivers. According to the Guardian, the self-driving cars’ computers would be a target for hackers. There are so many situations where a self-driving car could be led to cause accidents, especially if the human behind the wheel has never learned to drive or is too distracted or too trusting in the vehicle’s technology to intervene. A simple idea is that a hacker can encourage the cars to engage in a platooning pattern (where the cars line up closely behind each other like train cars), at which point the cars would be like a line of little ducklings ready to do the bidding of Mother Duck, leading them off the road or suddenly slamming on the brakes when the cars are very close and travelling at high speeds. 
Many proponents of self-driving cars act on the presumption that vehicle computers have an understanding of the environment around them, and in fact would be even more aware than a distracted human would be. Sure, if they’re equipped with the right sensors they’ll know the speeds of nearby cars, they’ll acknowledge the possibility that a child or deer could run onto the road at any moment, and they’ll probably monitor weather conditions. But in terms of anticipating the behavior of other drivers, they fall short. When I drive, I can look at other vehicles and anticipate their behavior. This driver is turned around looking at their kid in the backseat. This person’s truck has tree branches in the back that aren’t tied down. This person’s car has been rear-ended several times and the bumper is being held on with duct tape. Each of these pieces of information influences what I expect the driver to act like. A computer isn’t going to look at its surroundings and judge everything like this. As such, a computer will not be as well-informed or adaptive as humans, because we will never be able to anticipate all the different types of drivers that the computer would need to be ready to judge. 
Overall, there is no debate that a self-driving car would behave differently from a human-driven one. Much of this discrepancy is due to the fact that computers are endowed with the ability to be truly conscious and can be easily mislead or misinformed.

output_text:
Society remains divided on the issue of self-driving cars. Some people believe they are beneficial: they could reduce accidents, free up drivers’ time, and eliminate the need for detailed driving knowledge, enabling students to focus on other subjects in school and granting more freedom to individuals who previously could not drive alone, such as the disabled and elderly. Meanwhile, others highlight the challenges. Computers may not make decisions and adapt like attentive human drivers, present more security risks, and have a significant potential for vehicle failure. I align with this latter perspective, mainly because computers have more vulnerabilities and potential points of failure than humans.

Consider a story that illustrates the fallibility of computer systems in vehicles. In April, my parents and I were in Florida visiting my grandparents. We had a rental car, and my father was driving on the highway. A car ahead of us was going well below the speed limit, so my father accelerated to pass it. The rental car's computer mistakenly determined that he intended to brake instead of accelerate. Consequently, the car abruptly stopped on the busy highway, causing all passengers to be thrown forward and the car behind us to nearly rear-end us. This incident involved no human error or distracted driving—just a system trusted and deployed into millions of cars, activating without the driver’s knowledge or consent. This “feature” is indicative of the challenges we may face with fully autonomous vehicles.

Autonomous vehicles pose even greater risks when considering not just faulty software but also security vulnerabilities and how they manage driving in diverse environmental conditions alongside other drivers. According to The Guardian, self-driving cars’ computers are potential targets for hackers. Numerous situations exist where a self-driving car could be manipulated into causing accidents, especially if the human occupant has never learned to drive or is too distracted or overly trusting of the vehicle’s technology to intervene. For example, a hacker could manipulate cars to engage in a platooning pattern (where cars line up closely behind each other like train cars), making them susceptible to accidents if led off the road or if they suddenly brake while traveling at high speeds.

Many advocates of self-driving cars assume that vehicle computers possess an understanding of their environment and can be more aware than a distracted human. Equipped with the right sensors, they might detect the speeds of nearby cars, assess the risk of a child or deer darting onto the road, and monitor weather conditions. However, they fall short in anticipating other drivers' behavior. When I drive, I can observe other vehicles and predict their actions. This driver is turned around, looking at their child in the backseat. This person's truck has unsecured tree branches in the back. This individual’s car has been rear-ended multiple times, with a bumper held on by duct tape. Each piece of information shapes my expectations of how the driver will behave. A computer will not assess its surroundings and make judgments in this manner. Thus, it lacks the informed adaptability of humans, as we cannot predict all the different types of drivers the computer would need to evaluate.

In conclusion, there is no denying that a self-driving car operates differently from a human-driven one. This discrepancy largely stems from the fact that computers, unlike humans, lack true consciousness and can be easily misled or misinformed. The potential risks and vulnerabilities associated with self-driving cars outweigh their benefits, making them an unreliable substitute for human judgment and adaptability on the road.

output_text:
The self-driving car represents a revolutionary advancement in technology, but its integration into everyday life in America raises significant questions about feasibility. While the potential benefits of self-driving cars are considerable—such as enhanced safety through reduced accidents, elimination of driver distractions, improved traffic management, and savings in time and money—there are also notable disadvantages. These include dependency on weather conditions, inability to interpret human traffic signals, risks of malfunctioning, reliance on underdeveloped technologies, and potential job losses in transportation-related sectors. Importantly, there is no conclusive evidence that self-driving cars are inherently safer than traditional vehicles.

Presently, I believe it is premature to introduce self-driving cars into society. We need to advance our technological capabilities to ensure a seamless market introduction with minimal disruption to existing industries. Although self-driving cars hold the potential for significant societal benefits, the timing is not yet right.

If self-driving cars were introduced today, numerous challenges would arise. Their high cost—exceeding $100,000—means only a small fraction of the population could afford them, compounded by the necessity for specialized training to operate them safely. Moreover, the widespread adoption required for self-driving cars to be effective is unlikely if they remain accessible only to the wealthiest individuals. Security concerns are also paramount, as these vehicles could become targets for hackers, potentially compromising personal data.

Even if the cost of self-driving cars were reduced to make them accessible to the general public, the economic impact could be severe. Jobs in the transportation sector could be eliminated, the gasoline industry might suffer if these cars are predominantly electric, driver's education courses could become obsolete, and personal injury lawyers might face reduced demand due to fewer accidents. Such changes could be detrimental to many livelihoods and adversely affect the economy.

Furthermore, the arguments in favor of self-driving cars often overlook these broader social and economic consequences. While proponents highlight the potential for safety and efficiency, they frequently underestimate the challenges of securing universal accessibility, ensuring cybersecurity, and mitigating economic disruptions. Until these issues are addressed, it is neither practical nor advisable to strive for widespread adoption of self-driving cars. Thus, it is both unfeasible to expect mass public use of self-driving cars currently, and in the unlikely event of universal adoption, the economic repercussions could be significant.

Cars may also not be able to function in bad weather, as heavy precipitation could result in damage to integral sensors needed for the self driving functionality. The cars may not be able to recognize things human could (human turn signals for example). Removing the human removes all of the risk of a human driver, but also all of the potential problem solving that humans could perform.

Self-driving cars are a topic of much interest and debate, with opinions divided on their potential impact on society. While these vehicles promise to revolutionize transportation by eliminating human error and saving time, significant challenges remain in their development and implementation. This essay argues in favor of self-driving cars, highlighting their potential benefits while acknowledging the concerns that need to be addressed.

The primary advantage of self-driving cars is their potential to drastically reduce accidents caused by human error. By removing the driver from the equation, these vehicles can eliminate the risks associated with drunk driving and other dangerous behaviors. If executed correctly, self-driving technology could save countless lives by preventing vehicle-related incidents.

However, the removal of human drivers also presents challenges. Without human judgment, self-driving cars might prioritize efficiency over safety, choosing routes that are faster but potentially more hazardous for passengers. Additionally, these vehicles may struggle to operate in adverse weather conditions, as heavy precipitation could impair the sensors critical to their function. Furthermore, certain human nuances, such as interpreting non-verbal cues like turn signals, may be difficult for automated systems to recognize. While eliminating the human factor reduces risks, it also removes the problem-solving abilities that humans possess.

Despite these concerns, I am a strong proponent of self-driving cars. Personally, I am not confident in my driving skills, and I believe that developing self-driving technology poses an exciting challenge for computer science. For instance, creating a self-driving car requires advanced computer vision systems capable of recognizing other vehicles, measuring distances, and identifying road hazards.

Moreover, self-driving cars cater to the needs of both the industrious and the laid-back. They offer the opportunity to maximize productivity by freeing up time otherwise spent driving, making daily life more convenient. As such, I support the continued development of self-driving cars, confident that they herald a brighter, more efficient future for transportation.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars present certain challenges, their potential to enhance safety and efficiency makes them a promising innovation. By addressing the concerns associated with their development, we can embrace a future where transportation is safer, more efficient, and more accessible for all.

The day someone learns how to drive is a turning point in their lives and the lives of their family members. The new driver has a freshly established sense of independence and their family members no longer have to carpool them to wherever they need to go. However, with this privilege, comes lots of responsibility and potential danger. For this reason, and several others, self-driving cars are starting to make an appearance. Do these self-driving cars – the “ideal motorists” who do not get distracted the way current drivers do, are programmed to react immediately to cars in close proximity, and have the potential to significantly decrease traffic – truly make roads safer, or is this a fad that will soon be out of style? Due to the education required to operate a self-driving car, the necessary compliance among a majority of Americans to see the positive effects, and the relinquishing the power that human emotion and decision-making has, self-driving cars would serve as more of a problem than a solution. 
	Between logging a certain amount of hours as a student-driver and ultimately passing the driving test, the process to be able to legally drive can be long and grueling. Requiring even more training for individuals to operate a self-driving car may be intriguing for some, but unrealistic for most. At the same time, while the younger generations are becoming more tech-savvy, older adults and elders can struggle with operating technology. How are those who are confused on how to use an iPhone or computer expected to fully comprehend and operate a self-driving car? Likewise, if a person is still responsible for operating a self-driving car, then that defeats the purpose of self-driving cars allowing the passengers to direct their attention elsewhere when in a car. The idea that a majority of America’s drivers would take the time to learn how to work a self-driving car, and be able to do it successfully, nonetheless, is impractical. 
	In a perfect world, if every American with a car owned and effectively drove a self-driving car, the roads would likely be safer, traffic would be decreased, and police officers could spend their time focusing on more pressing issues other than enforcing speed limits. However, with the amount of people that have little interest in self-driving cars, whether it is because of their price, required training, or simply the absence of their significant need, the lack of compliance with this movement will make these potential positive effects highly unlikely to see. In fact, having a mix of both regular cars and self-driving cars sharing the road could be confusing and problematic. 
	While robots and technology can sometimes be proven to be smarter than the human mind, the one thing they lack is human emotion and rational thinking. In driving, both of these play a major role. Whether a person decides they want to change the route to their destination or has to react instantaneously to something on the road, among other situations, drivers are constantly putting a lot of thought into driving. Giving a robotic-run car essentially full control of all of this, especially when it is expected to keep everyone in the car safe, is taking a leap of faith – one that may be too risky to take. Although there is most certainly a need for safer roads and more focused driving, the training, compliance and lack of human input allowed with self-driving cars makes it unlikely that they will be the future of the American roads.
